facts.fact: Stripe uses date-based rolling versions (e.g., 2017-05-24) with automatic account pinning to the most recent version at first request, enabling ~100 backwards-incompatible upgrades since 2011 while maintaining full compatibility
GitHub API versions follow the same date-based naming scheme, with previous versions supported for at least 24 months after a new version is released
Cursor-based pagination is 17x faster than offset pagination when accessing deep pages (900,000+ rows), executing in 41ms vs 704ms on a 1-million-record dataset

Cursor-based pagination prevents duplicate and skipped items under concurrent writes because it uses a reference point rather than counting rows, unlike offset pagination which must scan and discard all preceding rows
Contentful moved its entire content API platform to cursor-based pagination due to inefficiency of offset pagination in dynamic environments where entries are constantly created, updated, and deleted
Maximum REST API resource nesting depth should be limited to 2 (occasionally 3) to avoid overly complex URLs, especially with UUID identifiers
Google Cloud APIs use channel-based versioning with three stability levels: alpha, beta, and stable; beta deprecations should persist for approximately 180 days before removal
HTTP 201 Created requires including a Location header with the URI of the newly created resource; returning 200 OK for POST creation is semantically incorrect as it doesn't indicate resource creation
HTTP 204 No Content must have an empty response body per RFC specification; sending 204 with JSON body violates HTTP semantics
HTTP 422 Unprocessable Entity indicates semantic validation errors (invalid field values, missing parameters) while 400 Bad Request indicates syntax errors (malformed JSON, missing headers)
HTTP 409 Conflict with optimistic locking uses ETags or version numbers; server must validate If-Match headers and return 409 if resource has been modified since the client's last read
HTTP 429 Too Many Requests Retry-After header can be specified as seconds (e.g., 'Retry-After: 120') or as an HTTP date (e.g., 'Retry-After: Thu, 16 Nov 2024 10:00:00 GMT')
Rate-limiting best practice: include X-RateLimit-Remaining on every response (not just 429s) so clients can self-throttle before hitting the limit
HTTP 202 Accepted is for asynchronous processing; the server must return either a Location header or status URL in the response body so clients can poll for results
OpenAPI Generator supports 50+ client generators and achieves 600k weekly NPM downloads; oapi-codegen moved from deepmap organization to its own organization in May 2024

Idempotent operations can legitimately return different HTTP status codes on retries; a 409 Conflict can be returned on a duplicate idempotent request if detected via idempotency key, even though the operation succeeded the first time

examples.name: Stripe API Versioning
examples.context: Payment platform managing ~100 backwards-incompatible upgrades since 2011
examples.detail: Date-based versioning with automatic account pinning. When breaking changes like replacing a boolean with a status field occur, Stripe applies version-change modules server-side that downgrade responses to maintain compatibility

examples.name: GitHub API Deprecation
examples.context: Deprecating password authentication and Authorizations API
examples.detail: 24-month support window after new version release; used X-GitHub-Api-Version header for explicit version specification

examples.name: Contentful Cursor Migration
examples.context: Large content management platform with high concurrency
examples.detail: Migrated entire REST and GraphQL APIs from offset to cursor pagination due to performance degradation under continuous create/update/delete load

examples.name: Concurrent Editor Conflict (409)
examples.context: CMS with multiple editors on same document
examples.detail: Two authors save edits simultaneously; second save returns 409 with current version number, client refreshes and retries with If-Match header containing latest ETag

analogies.concept: HTTP 201 Created vs 200 OK for POST
analogies.analogy: Receiving a package: 201 is 'your package was successfully delivered' with a tracking number (Location header). 200 is 'your request was successful' without confirming what was created. Client needs the Location header to know where to find the new resource.

analogies.concept: HTTP 204 No Content vs 200 OK for DELETE
analogies.analogy: Deleting a file: 204 means 'the file is gone, nothing left to report.' 200 with empty body is confusing—it suggests content exists but is just empty. 204 is semantically precise.

Offset is like 'skip the first 1000 items and get the next 10'—expensive if the list keeps growing. Cursor is like a bookmark: 'give me the next 10 items after this marker'—works even as the list changes.

Editing a shared spreadsheet: your client doesn't lock rows. Instead, it reads a version number, makes changes offline, then tries to save with 'save only if version still matches.' If someone else updated it, you get 409 and must refresh.

analogies.concept: HTTP 422 Unprocessable Entity vs 400 Bad Request
analogies.analogy: 400 is 'I can't read your handwriting' (syntax error). 422 is 'I read your handwriting fine, but you wrote an invalid zip code' (semantic error).

Like a newspaper archive: old editions remain available indefinitely. Readers stay on the edition they first subscribed to until they explicitly upgrade. Breaking changes appear in new dated editions; old editions continue unchanged.
Dropping off laundry: you get a claim ticket (job ID) and told when to pick it up (Retry-After). You don't wait for the results; you check back later using the ticket.

key_numbers.context: Beyond this, URLs become unwieldy and N+1 query problems emerge
After a new version is released, the previous version remains supported for at least 24 months
Beta deprecations should remain available for approximately 180 days before removal
Via openapi-generator-cli, plus 30M via Docker image
Successfully deployed since 2011 while maintaining full compatibility with every prior version
6 months announcement, 12 months active migration support, 18-24 months total before removal

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP 200 OK can be used for any successful operation, including validations errors or permission failures
common_misconceptions.reality: 200 OK should only be used when the request truly succeeds. Validation errors should return 422, permission failures 403, conflicts 409. Returning 200 for failures hides issues and breaks observability.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Clients can't distinguish success from failure by status code alone; must parse response body, breaking HTTP semantics

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP 201 Created and 200 OK are interchangeable for POST requests
common_misconceptions.reality: 201 Created explicitly signals a new resource was created and must include Location header. 200 OK is for successful requests that don't necessarily create a resource (e.g., status update, search).
common_misconceptions.consequence: Clients don't know where to access the newly created resource if Location header is missing

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP 204 No Content can include a JSON response body
common_misconceptions.reality: 204 is defined by RFC 9110 to have no content. Including a body violates the specification and breaks clients expecting an empty response.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Clients parsing the empty body will encounter JSON parsing errors or unexpected behavior

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP 400 Bad Request and 422 Unprocessable Entity are the same thing
common_misconceptions.reality: 400 is for syntax errors (malformed JSON, invalid headers). 422 is for semantic errors (validation failures, invalid field values). They address different problems.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Clients can't distinguish whether to fix request format vs request content; different remediation strategies

common_misconceptions.misconception: Offset pagination scales well even with millions of rows
common_misconceptions.reality: Offset pagination requires scanning and discarding all rows before the offset, becoming 10-100x slower at deep pages. Cursor pagination maintains consistent O(1) performance.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Performance degrades catastrophically for deep pagination; cursor-based is non-negotiable for large datasets

common_misconceptions.misconception: Offset pagination correctly handles concurrent inserts and deletes
common_misconceptions.reality: Offset pagination causes duplicate or skipped items when data changes between requests. Cursor pagination is stable under concurrent writes because it uses a reference point, not a count.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Users see duplicate entries or entries get skipped in feeds/event streams with concurrent write activity

common_misconceptions.misconception: REST resources can be arbitrarily nested (e.g., /orgs/{id}/teams/{id}/members/{id}/projects/{id}/tasks)
common_misconceptions.reality: Beyond 2-3 levels, nesting creates N+1 query problems, fragile URLs vulnerable to relationship changes, and complex URL parsing. Root resources are more maintainable.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Excessive database queries per request; URLs break if resource ownership changes; difficult to query a resource without knowing its entire hierarchy

common_misconceptions.misconception: API versioning requires v1, v2, v3 in the URL path
common_misconceptions.reality: Date-based versioning (Stripe), channel-based versioning (Google), and header-based versioning (GitHub) all work better. Sequential versioning conflates semantic changes with timeline.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Sequential versioning creates artificial version numbers that don't map to real changes; clients don't know what changed between versions

common_misconceptions.misconception: Deprecation can happen immediately upon release of a new API version
common_misconceptions.reality: Industry standard is 6-24 months of overlap support. GitHub provides 24 months, Google Cloud recommends 180 days for beta. Immediate removal breaks integrations.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Clients are forced to upgrade on short notice, causing downtime or incompatible dependencies

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP 429 Too Many Requests doesn't need a Retry-After header if the client uses exponential backoff
common_misconceptions.reality: Retry-After is mandatory. Without it, naive clients hammer the server in synchronized bursts. With it, clients can respect server hints. Exponential backoff without Retry-After causes thundering herd.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Server gets pounded harder during recovery; distributed denial of service risk from synchronized retries

common_misconceptions.misconception: Rate limit information only matters in 429 responses
common_misconceptions.reality: Including X-RateLimit-Remaining in every response (2xx and 4xx) lets clients self-throttle before hitting the limit.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Clients overshoot the limit and waste requests; better client behavior possible with proactive rate-limit data

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP 409 Conflict is only for database constraint violations
common_misconceptions.reality: 409 is for any state conflict: concurrent updates (optimistic locking), state machine violations (publishing already-published resource), duplicate unique constraints. It's a broad semantic error.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Unclear semantics; client doesn't know what action to take (retry vs refresh vs abort)

common_misconceptions.misconception: OpenAPI specs are mostly documentation; client generation is a 'nice-to-have'
common_misconceptions.reality: OpenAPI with proper client generation (via openapi-generator, oapi-codegen, Hey API) eliminates 50% of API integration bugs. Type-safe clients prevent schema mismatches.
common_misconceptions.consequence: Manual client implementation introduces field name typos, type mismatches, missing error handling; higher bug density
